Open-vocabulary vs closed-vocabulary object detection¶

This notebook is the stakeholder-facing justification for the model choice.

The earlier notebook already compared the open-vocabulary models and selected Grounding DINO as the strongest open-vocabulary candidate. The purpose of this notebook is narrower and more critical: does that choice still make sense when compared with strong closed-vocabulary detectors?

Reproducibility¶

This notebook is designed to run from the same folder that contains:

  • data/
  • stakeholder_eval.py
  • .hf_cache/ and .torch_cache/ if you want the first run to avoid re-downloading weights

If local caches are present, the code will use them. Otherwise the first run will download the pretrained weights from the official model repositories.

In [1]:
import sys
import subprocess
packages = ['numpy', 'pandas', 'matplotlib', 'pillow', 'torch', 'torchvision', 'transformers', 'ultralytics']
subprocess.run([sys.executable, '-m', 'pip', 'install', '-q', *packages], check=False)
Out[1]:
CompletedProcess(args=['C:\\Users\\gtomo\\AppData\\Local\\Programs\\Python\\Python313\\python.exe', '-m', 'pip', 'install', '-q', 'numpy', 'pandas', 'matplotlib', 'pillow', 'torch', 'torchvision', 'transformers', 'ultralytics'], returncode=0)

Evaluation design¶

The new data introduces two different questions, so the benchmark is split into two parts.

1. Shared-class benchmark¶

This is the fair comparison. It uses only classes that all models can detect with their native label space: apple and cup.

2. Novel-class stress test¶

This is the deployment relevance check. It uses pear, bucket, and egg, which appeared in the new images but are not part of the COCO label space used by the closed-vocabulary baselines.

These two sections should not be mixed. The first tests raw detection quality on shared vocabulary. The second tests task flexibility when the requested object list changes.

Why these metrics¶

  • Frame-level AP is the primary metric because the dataset does not contain bounding-box annotations. It measures whether frames with the target object are ranked above frames without it.
  • Precision / recall / F1 at a fixed threshold (0.25) show the practical operating trade-off between false positives and missed detections.
  • Latency per image matters because a deployment decision should consider runtime, not just accuracy.
  • Exact label-space support is reported separately because a closed-vocabulary detector cannot produce an exact class prediction if that class is absent from its pretrained taxonomy.

Critical scope note¶

Bounding-box mAP was not used here. That was a deliberate choice, not an omission. The folder does not contain box annotations, so computing box mAP would first require building a new labeled dataset. That would change the task from model comparison to annotation production. The evaluation below instead focuses on the decision the stakeholder actually cares about: which model is better suited to this evolving object-detection requirement?

In [2]:
from pathlib import Path
import json
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import Markdown, display, Image

from stakeholder_eval import RESULTS_DIR, run_experiment
In [3]:
force_run = not (RESULTS_DIR / 'summary_metrics.csv').exists()
summary = run_experiment(force=force_run)
summary

Dataset overview¶

In [4]:
display(Image(filename='data_contact_sheet_numbered.jpg'))

The sequence now contains:

  • the original office occlusion sequence,
  • new tabletop scenes with pear, bucket, and apple,
  • white-table scenes with cup and egg,
  • bright and low-light office scenes with changed viewpoint and lighting.

This matters because it lets us separate known-category robustness from vocabulary flexibility.

Exact label-space support¶

In [5]:
support_df = pd.read_csv(RESULTS_DIR / 'class_support.csv')
support_df
Out[5]:
class Grounding DINO Base YOLO11n RT-DETRv2-R18 Faster R-CNN ResNet50 FPN V2
0 apple 1 1 1 1
1 cup 1 1 1 1
2 pear 1 0 0 0
3 bucket 1 0 0 0
4 egg 1 0 0 0
In [6]:
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'class_support.png')))

This table is important for interpretation. If a model does not contain an exact class such as pear, bucket, or egg, then any failure on that class is a taxonomy limitation, not only a detector-quality issue.

Shared-class benchmark¶

In [7]:
summary_df = pd.read_csv(RESULTS_DIR / 'summary_metrics.csv')
shared_df = summary_df[summary_df['class'].isin(['apple', 'cup'])].copy()
shared_df[['model', 'class', 'frame_ap', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1', 'latency_per_image_s', 'class_supported']].sort_values(['class', 'frame_ap', 'f1'], ascending=[True, False, False])

display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'shared_summary.png')))
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'shared_apple_trace.png')))
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'shared_cup_trace.png')))

Interpretation of the shared benchmark¶

  • This is the fairest accuracy comparison because all compared models are allowed to detect these classes.
  • If a closed-vocabulary model wins here, that does not automatically invalidate Grounding DINO. It only means that for a fixed known taxonomy, a specialized detector can be more efficient.
  • This section should be used to answer: If the object list never changes, what is the best detector?

Novel-class stress test¶

In [9]:
novel_df = summary_df[summary_df['class'].isin(['pear', 'bucket', 'egg'])].copy()
novel_df[['model', 'class', 'frame_ap', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1', 'latency_per_image_s', 'class_supported']].sort_values(['class', 'frame_ap', 'f1'], ascending=[True, False, False])

In [10]:
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'novel_summary.png')))
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'novel_pear_trace.png')))
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'novel_bucket_trace.png')))
display(Image(filename=str(RESULTS_DIR / 'novel_egg_trace.png')))

Interpretation of the novel-class stress test¶

  • This is not a fair closed-vocabulary benchmark. It is a task-fit benchmark.
  • The purpose is to test the exact stakeholder risk: what happens when new objects appear without retraining.
  • Using proxy labels such as sports ball, bowl, or orange would artificially improve closed-vocabulary models while changing the semantic task, so proxies were intentionally not used.

Final conclusion¶

In [11]:
report = Path(RESULTS_DIR / 'report.md').read_text(encoding='utf-8')
display(Markdown(report))

Stakeholder comparison: open-vocabulary vs closed-vocabulary detectors¶

What this notebook tests¶

  • A fair shared-class benchmark on apple and cup, because those classes are supported by all compared models.
  • A novel-class stress test on pear, bucket, and egg, because these appeared in the updated data but are outside the standard COCO label space used by the closed-vocabulary baselines.
  • A practical deployment trade-off: ranking quality, operational precision/recall at one threshold, and inference latency.

Why these metrics were used¶

  • Frame-level AP is the primary ranking metric because we do not have bounding-box annotations. It tests whether frames containing the target object are ranked above frames that do not.
  • Precision, recall, and F1 at threshold 0.25 show the operational trade-off between false alarms and misses.
  • Latency per image matters because a detector that wins slightly on accuracy but is much slower may be a poor deployment choice.
  • Exact label-space support is reported separately, because a closed-vocabulary detector can only detect classes that exist in its pretrained taxonomy.

Critical interpretation¶

  • Best closed-vocabulary model on the shared benchmark: Faster R-CNN ResNet50 FPN V2.
  • Fastest model on the shared benchmark: YOLO11n.
  • The shared benchmark tells us how well each model handles known categories under occlusion, viewpoint change, and lighting change.
  • The novel-class benchmark answers a different question: what happens when the task vocabulary changes without retraining.
  • A closed-vocabulary model scoring zero on pear, bucket, or egg is not a bug in the metric. It reflects a real deployment limitation: the requested class does not exist in the model label set.

csv
model,frame_ap,precision,recall,f1,latency_per_image_s

Faster R-CNN ResNet50 FPN V2,0.9946,0.869,0.9756,0.9172,5.9251

Exact label-space support¶

csv
class,Grounding DINO Base,YOLO11n,RT-DETRv2-R18,Faster R-CNN ResNet50 FPN V2

apple,1,1,1,1

cup,1,1,1,1

pear,1,0,0,0

bucket,1,0,0,0

egg,1,0,0,0

Outcome¶

  • If the vocabulary is fixed to a known set such as apple and cup, a closed-vocabulary detector is competitive and may even be preferable for speed.
  • If the deployment requires adapting to new requested objects without retraining, the open-vocabulary reference remains the safer choice.
  • This means the final recommendation depends on the real system requirement: fixed taxonomy favors a closed-vocabulary baseline, evolving task vocabulary favors Grounding DINO.

Sources¶

  • Grounding DINO Base: https://huggingface.co/IDEA-Research/grounding-dino-base
  • YOLO11n: https://docs.ultralytics.com/models/yolo11/
  • RT-DETRv2-R18: https://huggingface.co/PekingU/rtdetr_v2_r18vd
  • Faster R-CNN ResNet50 FPN V2: https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/master/models/generated/torchvision.models.detection.fasterrcnn_resnet50_fpn_v2.html

Recommendation wording for the stakeholder¶

A careful conclusion is:

Closed-vocabulary baselines are competitive and in some cases better on shared known classes, especially when speed matters. However, the expanded dataset shows that the deployment requirement is not a fixed closed taxonomy. Because the object list changes across scenes, the final system still benefits from an open-vocabulary detector, and Grounding DINO remains the safer choice despite the higher runtime cost.
